questions.question: What is middleware in ASP.NET Core and how does the request pipeline work?
questions.answer:
Middleware is software that's assembled into an application pipeline to handle requests and responses. Each middleware component can: process the request, pass it to the next component, process the response on the way back.

The pipeline is a chain of middleware — each has a reference to the next. Think of it as a series of delegates where each can short-circuit (return early) or continue by calling next().

// Middleware execution order (declared order matters)
app.UseExceptionHandler();   // 1st: wraps everything, catches exceptions
app.UseHsts();               // 2nd
app.UseHttpsRedirection();   // 3rd
app.UseStaticFiles();        // 4th: serves files, short-circuits if found
app.UseRouting();            // 5th: matches URL to endpoint
app.UseCors();               // 6th: CORS must be BEFORE auth
app.UseAuthentication();     // 7th: sets HttpContext.User
app.UseAuthorization();      // 8th: checks policies/roles
app.UseRateLimiter();        // 9th
app.MapControllers();        // endpoint: the actual controller actions

Request flow: Request enters at top (ExceptionHandler), passes through each middleware, reaches the controller action, then the response travels back UP through the same middleware chain in reverse.
followUps: What is the difference between Use(), Run(), and Map()?
What happens if middleware doesn't call next()?

followUpAnswers.What is the difference between Use(), Ru:
These three methods are the building blocks of the ASP.NET Core middleware pipeline, and each has a distinct role:

Use() — middleware that participates in the full request/response cycle:

// Use() adds a middleware that CAN call the next delegate
// It has access to both the incoming request and the outgoing response
app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    // --- Request phase (before next) ---
    var sw = Stopwatch.StartNew();
    Console.WriteLine($"Request: {context.Request.Method} {context.Request.Path}");

    await next(context);  // call the next middleware in the chain

    // --- Response phase (after next returns) ---
    sw.Stop();
    Console.WriteLine($"Response: {context.Response.StatusCode} in {sw.ElapsedMilliseconds}ms");
});

Run() — terminal middleware that ends the pipeline:

// Run() does NOT receive a next delegate — it CANNOT call the next middleware
// It terminates the pipeline. Anything registered after Run() is NEVER called.
app.Run(async context =>
{
    context.Response.ContentType = "text/plain";
    await context.Response.WriteAsync("Pipeline ends here — nothing after me runs");
});

// This would NEVER execute:
app.Use(async (ctx, next) => { /* dead code */ });

Map() — branches the pipeline based on request path prefix:

// Requests to /api/** go into the api sub-pipeline
app.Map("/api", apiApp =>
{
    apiApp.UseAuthentication();
    apiApp.UseAuthorization();
    apiApp.MapControllers();
    // Branch ends here — does NOT rejoin the main pipeline
});

// Requests to /health go into their own sub-pipeline
app.Map("/health", healthApp =>
{
    healthApp.Run(async ctx =>
        await ctx.Response.WriteAsync("Healthy"));
});

// Requests matching NEITHER /api nor /health reach here
app.Run(async ctx => await ctx.Response.WriteAsync("Not Found"));

Key differences summary:

MethodCalls next?Branches?Common use
Use()Yes (can choose not to)NoLogging, auth, CORS, request modification
Run()NeverNoFinal fallback handler, health check terminal
Map()N/A (creates sub-pipeline)Yes, permanentlySub-pipelines per path prefix (API vs MVC vs health)
followUpAnswers.What happens if middleware doesn't call:
When a middleware does not call await next(context), it short-circuits the pipeline. No subsequent middleware — including the controller action — receives the request. The response written by that middleware is returned directly to the client.

This is intentional behavior and is used in several important scenarios:

// Example 1: Authentication guard — short-circuit if no valid token
app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    var token = context.Request.Headers["Authorization"].FirstOrDefault();
    if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(token) || !IsValidToken(token))
    {
        context.Response.StatusCode = 401;
        await context.Response.WriteAsJsonAsync(new { error = "Unauthorized" });
        return;  // Short-circuit — next() is NOT called
    }

    await next(context);  // Only called if token is valid
});

// Example 2: UseStaticFiles — short-circuits if file exists on disk
// app.UseStaticFiles() internally does:
app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    var filePath = MapToPhysicalPath(context.Request.Path);
    if (File.Exists(filePath))
    {
        // Serve the file and return — controller never sees this request
        await context.Response.SendFileAsync(filePath);
        return;  // Short-circuit
    }
    await next(context);  // Pass to next middleware if file not found
});

// Example 3: Rate limiter — reject over-limit requests
app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    if (!_rateLimiter.TryAcquire(context.Connection.RemoteIpAddress))
    {
        context.Response.StatusCode = 429;
        context.Response.Headers["Retry-After"] = "60";
        return;  // Short-circuit
    }
    await next(context);
});

Important considerations when short-circuiting:

  • Always set an appropriate HTTP status code before returning — the default is 200 if you forget
  • Make sure context.Response.HasStarted is false before writing — once headers are sent you can't change the status code
  • Middleware registered after the short-circuiting middleware in Program.cs is completely skipped — including exception handling middleware if it was registered after
  • Exception handler middleware (registered first) still wraps the entire chain — so even short-circuiting exceptions are caught by it

The short-circuit pattern is a deliberate performance optimization: UseStaticFiles serves millions of static file requests without ever hitting the router, auth, or controllers.

questions.tip: Order matters critically. CORS before Auth (CORS preflight must not require authentication). ExceptionHandler first (wraps everything). Routing before Auth (auth needs to know which endpoint was matched for endpoint-level authorization policies).

questions.question: How do you create custom middleware in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
Two ways: convention-based class or IMiddleware interface (supports DI):

Convention-based (most common):

public class RequestLoggingMiddleware
{
    private readonly RequestDelegate _next;
    private readonly ILogger<RequestLoggingMiddleware> _logger;

    public RequestLoggingMiddleware(
        RequestDelegate next,
        ILogger<RequestLoggingMiddleware> logger)
    {
        _next = next;
        _logger = logger;
    }

    public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext context)
    {
        _logger.LogInformation("{Method} {Path} started",
            context.Request.Method, context.Request.Path);

        await _next(context);  // call next middleware

        _logger.LogInformation("{Method} {Path} → {Status}",
            context.Request.Method, context.Request.Path,
            context.Response.StatusCode);
    }
}

// Extension method for clean registration
public static class RequestLoggingMiddlewareExtensions
{
    public static IApplicationBuilder UseRequestLogging(
        this IApplicationBuilder app)
        => app.UseMiddleware<RequestLoggingMiddleware>();
}

// Program.cs
app.UseRequestLogging();

IMiddleware interface — activated per-request from DI container. Better when the middleware needs scoped services (DbContext, etc.) because it's re-instantiated per request.
followUps: When would you use IMiddleware over convention-based middleware?
How do you short-circuit the pipeline?

followUpAnswers.When would you use IMiddleware over conv:
The fundamental difference is lifetime and DI scope. Understanding this distinction is what interviewers are testing.

Convention-based middleware is instantiated once at application startup (singleton lifetime). Its constructor dependencies are also resolved once — which means it can only safely take singleton services.

// Convention-based: constructed ONCE at startup
public class RequestLoggingMiddleware
{
    private readonly RequestDelegate _next;
    private readonly ILogger<RequestLoggingMiddleware> _logger; // OK: ILogger is singleton-safe

    // PROBLEM: cannot inject IDbContext here — it's scoped!
    // The ctor runs once; the scoped service would be the same instance across all requests
    public RequestLoggingMiddleware(RequestDelegate next, ILogger<RequestLoggingMiddleware> logger)
    {
        _next = next;
        _logger = logger;
    }

    // Workaround: inject scoped services as InvokeAsync parameters — framework resolves per-request
    public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext context, IUserRepository userRepo)
    {
        // userRepo is scoped — correctly resolved per request via InvokeAsync parameter injection
        var user = await userRepo.GetCurrentUserAsync(context.User);
        await _next(context);
    }
}

IMiddleware is activated from the DI container on every request — it gets a fresh instance per request, enabling proper scoped service injection in the constructor:

// IMiddleware: re-activated per request from DI container
public class AuditMiddleware : IMiddleware
{
    private readonly AuditDbContext _db;    // Scoped — safe in constructor!
    private readonly ICurrentUserService _user; // Scoped — safe!

    public AuditMiddleware(AuditDbContext db, ICurrentUserService user)
    {
        _db = db;
        _user = user;
    }

    public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext context, RequestDelegate next)
    {
        await next(context);

        // Log every request to the audit DB — scoped EF context works correctly
        _db.AuditLogs.Add(new AuditLog
        {
            UserId = _user.Id,
            Path = context.Request.Path,
            StatusCode = context.Response.StatusCode,
            Timestamp = DateTime.UtcNow
        });
        await _db.SaveChangesAsync();
    }
}

// Registration: middleware must be registered in DI as well
builder.Services.AddScoped<AuditMiddleware>(); // scoped because it has scoped deps
app.UseMiddleware<AuditMiddleware>();

Decision guide:

Use caseApproach
Only needs singleton deps (ILogger, IConfiguration, IOptions)Convention-based
Needs scoped deps (DbContext, current user service)IMiddleware OR InvokeAsync parameter injection
Complex construction logicIMiddleware (cleaner)
Simple inline logicapp.Use(async (ctx, next) => { ... })
followUpAnswers.How do you short-circuit the pipeline?:
Short-circuiting means responding to the client without calling await next(context), which prevents all downstream middleware and controller actions from executing.

The pattern:

public class ApiKeyMiddleware
{
    private readonly RequestDelegate _next;
    private readonly string _apiKey;

    public ApiKeyMiddleware(RequestDelegate next, IConfiguration config)
    {
        _next = next;
        _apiKey = config["ApiKey"]!;
    }

    public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext context)
    {
        // Attempt to get the API key from the request
        if (!context.Request.Headers.TryGetValue("X-Api-Key", out var providedKey)
            || providedKey != _apiKey)
        {
            // SHORT-CIRCUIT: write response and return WITHOUT calling _next
            context.Response.StatusCode = StatusCodes.Status401Unauthorized;
        
            // Best practice: use ProblemDetails for APIs
            await context.Response.WriteAsJsonAsync(new ProblemDetails
            {
                Status = 401,
                Title = "Unauthorized",
                Detail = "A valid API key is required."
            });

            return;  // <-- this is the short-circuit
        }

        // Valid key: pass to next middleware
        await _next(context);
    }
}

Practical examples of short-circuiting built into ASP.NET Core:

  • UseStaticFiles — short-circuits if the requested file exists on disk
  • UseAuthentication — does NOT short-circuit (it sets the user identity); UseAuthorization short-circuits with 401/403 if auth fails
  • UseRateLimiter — short-circuits with 429 if limit exceeded
  • UseResponseCaching — short-circuits with cached response if cache hit

Guard against double-response:

// Always check HasStarted before writing if you're in a post-next path
if (!context.Response.HasStarted)
{
    context.Response.StatusCode = 500;
    await context.Response.WriteAsync("Error");
}

Once Response.HasStarted is true (headers were flushed), you cannot change the status code or headers — attempting to do so throws an InvalidOperationException.

questions.tip: Short-circuit = don't call _next. If you return early without calling await _next(context), no downstream middleware executes. This is how UseStaticFiles works — if a file is found, it responds directly and never reaches your controllers.

questions.question: What is the difference between Use(), Run(), and Map() in ASP.NET Core middleware?
questions.answer:
Three methods for adding middleware components:

Use() — adds middleware that can call the next component. Most common.

app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    // before
    await next();   // calls next middleware
    // after (response flowing back)
});

Run() — terminal middleware. Does NOT call next — short-circuits the pipeline. Used at the end.

app.Run(async context =>
{
    await context.Response.WriteAsync("Hello from terminal middleware!");
    // No next() — pipeline ends here
});

Map() — branches the pipeline based on URL path. Each branch has its own middleware chain.

// All requests to /api/** go through ApiMiddleware only
app.Map("/api", apiApp =>
{
    apiApp.UseAuthentication();
    apiApp.UseAuthorization();
    apiApp.MapControllers();
});

// Requests NOT matching /api go here
app.Run(async context => await context.Response.WriteAsync("Not found"));

followUpAnswers.What is MapWhen()?:
MapWhen() branches the middleware pipeline based on a predicate on HttpContext — not just a path prefix. Like Map(), the branch is permanent: requests that match the predicate enter the branch and never return to the main pipeline.

// Branch for all requests that have a specific query parameter
app.MapWhen(
    predicate: context => context.Request.Query.ContainsKey("debug"),
    configuration: debugApp =>
    {
        debugApp.Use(async (ctx, next) =>
        {
            ctx.Response.Headers["X-Debug"] = "true";
            await next(ctx);
        });
        // This branch has its own isolated pipeline
        debugApp.Run(async ctx =>
            await ctx.Response.WriteAsJsonAsync(new
            {
                headers = ctx.Request.Headers.ToDictionary(h => h.Key, h => h.Value.ToString()),
                query = ctx.Request.Query.ToDictionary(q => q.Key, q => q.Value.ToString())
            }));
    });

// Requests WITHOUT ?debug never enter the branch above
app.MapControllers();  // normal requests go here

// Another practical example: branch on Content-Type
app.MapWhen(
    ctx => ctx.Request.ContentType?.Contains("application/xml") == true,
    xmlApp =>
    {
        xmlApp.UseMiddleware<XmlRequestParserMiddleware>();
        xmlApp.MapControllers();
    });

Key characteristic — permanent branching: Once a request enters a MapWhen branch, it does not rejoin the main pipeline after the branch completes. This is the same behavior as Map().

Predicate receives the full HttpContext: You can branch on any request property — headers, query strings, content type, user claims, remote IP — not just the path. This makes MapWhen more flexible than Map().

// Branch on authenticated user's role
app.MapWhen(
    ctx => ctx.User.IsInRole("Admin"),
    adminApp =>
    {
        adminApp.UseMiddleware<AdminAuditMiddleware>();
        adminApp.MapControllers();
    });
followUpAnswers.What is UseWhen()?:
UseWhen() conditionally adds middleware into the pipeline based on a predicate, but unlike MapWhen(), it rejoins the main pipeline after the conditional branch completes. Think of it as "inject extra middleware for matching requests, then continue normally."

// UseWhen: apply extra logging only for /api requests, then rejoin main pipeline
app.UseWhen(
    predicate: ctx => ctx.Request.Path.StartsWithSegments("/api"),
    configuration: apiApp =>
    {
        // This extra middleware only runs for /api requests
        apiApp.Use(async (ctx, next) =>
        {
            var correlationId = ctx.Request.Headers["X-Correlation-Id"]
                .FirstOrDefault() ?? Guid.NewGuid().ToString();
            ctx.Items["CorrelationId"] = correlationId;
            ctx.Response.Headers["X-Correlation-Id"] = correlationId;
            await next(ctx);
        });
        // After this branch, the request REJOINS the main pipeline
    });

// ALL requests (including /api ones) reach here after the conditional branch
app.UseAuthentication();
app.UseAuthorization();
app.MapControllers();

MapWhen vs UseWhen — the critical difference:

FeatureMapWhen()UseWhen()
Rejoins main pipeline?No — permanent branchYes — continues after branch
Use caseCompletely separate sub-pipelinesInject conditional middleware inline
After branchNothing from main pipeline runsMain pipeline continues normally
// WRONG — using MapWhen when you want UseWhen:
app.MapWhen(
    ctx => ctx.Request.Path.StartsWithSegments("/api"),
    apiApp => apiApp.UseMiddleware<CorrelationMiddleware>());
// BUG: MapControllers() in the main pipeline NEVER runs for /api requests!
app.MapControllers();  // dead code for /api requests

// CORRECT — UseWhen rejoins:
app.UseWhen(
    ctx => ctx.Request.Path.StartsWithSegments("/api"),
    apiApp => apiApp.UseMiddleware<CorrelationMiddleware>());
app.MapControllers();  // /api requests DO reach here after CorrelationMiddleware

Common UseWhen scenarios: Adding request ID headers for API routes only, enabling detailed request logging for specific paths, applying IP filtering middleware for admin routes while still routing through the main pipeline.

questions.tip: MapWhen vs UseWhen: MapWhen branches permanently (matched requests never return to the main pipeline). UseWhen conditionally adds middleware but rejoins the main pipeline after. Use MapWhen for separate sub-pipelines, UseWhen for conditional middleware injection.

questions.question: What are action filters in ASP.NET Core and when do you use them?
questions.answer:
Filters run at specific points in the MVC filter pipeline — after routing has selected the action but before/after the action executes. They're MVC-aware (have access to controller context, action descriptor, model state).

Filter types and execution order:

  1. Authorization filters — first; short-circuit if unauthorized. [Authorize]
  2. Resource filters — before model binding; good for caching.
  3. Action filters — before/after action executes. Most commonly customized.
  4. Exception filters — handle unhandled exceptions from actions.
  5. Result filters — before/after action result (IActionResult) executes.
public class ValidateModelFilter : IActionFilter
{
    public void OnActionExecuting(ActionExecutingContext context)
    {
        if (!context.ModelState.IsValid)
        {
            context.Result = new BadRequestObjectResult(
                new ValidationProblemDetails(context.ModelState));
        }
    }

    public void OnActionExecuted(ActionExecutedContext context) { }
}

Apply globally: builder.Services.AddControllers(o => o.Filters.Add<ValidateModelFilter>());
Per-controller or action: [ServiceFilter(typeof(ValidateModelFilter))]
followUps: What is the difference between middleware and action filters?

followUpAnswers.What is the difference between middlewar:
This is a fundamental ASP.NET Core architecture question. Both middleware and action filters intercept requests, but they operate at different layers with different capabilities.

Key differences:

AspectMiddlewareAction Filters
LayerHTTP pipeline (before MVC)MVC filter pipeline (inside MVC)
Runs forALL requests (static files, health checks, everything)Only MVC controller actions
MVC contextNot available (no ModelState, no ActionDescriptor)Full MVC context available
Access toHttpContext onlyModelState, ActionArguments, controller instance, route data
Short-circuitSet response directlySet context.Result to an IActionResult
LifetimeSingleton (convention) or per-request (IMiddleware)Transient, Scoped, or per-action via ServiceFilter
// MIDDLEWARE — best for cross-cutting HTTP concerns
app.Use(async (context, next) =>
{
    // context.GetEndpoint()?.Metadata — you CAN get endpoint metadata
    // but you cannot access ModelState, action arguments, controller type, etc.
    var requestId = Guid.NewGuid().ToString("N");
    context.Response.Headers["X-Request-Id"] = requestId;
    await next(context);
});

// ACTION FILTER — best for MVC-specific concerns
public class ValidateModelFilter : IActionFilter
{
    public void OnActionExecuting(ActionExecutingContext context)
    {
        // Rich MVC context:
        var controllerName = context.ActionDescriptor.RouteValues["controller"];
        var modelState = context.ModelState;        // full ModelState
        var arguments = context.ActionArguments;    // bound action parameters

        if (!modelState.IsValid)
        {
            // Short-circuit with a typed IActionResult
            context.Result = new UnprocessableEntityObjectResult(
                new ValidationProblemDetails(modelState));
        }
    }

    public void OnActionExecuted(ActionExecutedContext context)
    {
        // context.Result — the IActionResult the action returned
        // context.Exception — any exception thrown by the action
    }
}

Decision rule:

  • Use middleware for: request logging, CORS, rate limiting, authentication, HTTPS redirection, static files — concerns that apply to all HTTP traffic regardless of MVC
  • Use action filters for: model validation, response shaping, action-level logging with MVC context, auditing specific controller actions, cache headers per action
followUpAnswers.What is a global filter?:
A global filter is an action filter (or any MVC filter type) registered to run for every controller action in the application — without needing to apply an attribute to each controller or action individually.

Registering global filters:

// Program.cs — three ways to register global filters
builder.Services.AddControllers(options =>
{
    // 1. Instance registration (no DI support)
    options.Filters.Add(new ResponseCacheAttribute { Duration = 60 });

    // 2. Type registration — framework creates instance, no DI
    options.Filters.Add(typeof(ValidateModelFilter));

    // 3. Type registration with DI (preferred for filters with dependencies)
    options.Filters.Add<ValidateModelFilter>();
});

// For filters that need scoped/transient services from DI:
builder.Services.AddScoped<AuditFilter>();
builder.Services.AddControllers(options =>
{
    options.Filters.AddService<AuditFilter>();
    // or: options.Filters.AddService(typeof(AuditFilter));
});

Practical global filter examples:

// Global model validation filter — eliminates ModelState.IsValid checks in every action
public class AutoValidateModelFilter : IActionFilter
{
    public void OnActionExecuting(ActionExecutingContext context)
    {
        if (!context.ModelState.IsValid)
            context.Result = new UnprocessableEntityObjectResult(
                new ValidationProblemDetails(context.ModelState));
    }
    public void OnActionExecuted(ActionExecutedContext ctx) { }
}

// Global exception filter — catches unhandled exceptions from any action
public class GlobalExceptionFilter : IExceptionFilter
{
    private readonly ILogger<GlobalExceptionFilter> _logger;
    public GlobalExceptionFilter(ILogger<GlobalExceptionFilter> logger)
        => _logger = logger;

    public void OnException(ExceptionContext context)
    {
        _logger.LogError(context.Exception, "Unhandled action exception");
        context.Result = new ObjectResult(new ProblemDetails
        {
            Status = 500,
            Title = "An error occurred"
        }) { StatusCode = 500 };
        context.ExceptionHandled = true;
    }
}

Filter order for globals: Global filters run before controller-level filters, which run before action-level filters. You can control global filter order with the Order property — lower numbers run first in the pre-action phase and last in the post-action phase.

questions.tip: Use filters over middleware when you need MVC context (ModelState, controller/action names, route data). Use middleware for cross-cutting concerns that operate on raw HTTP (request logging, CORS, rate limiting).

questions.question: How do you implement global exception handling with middleware?
questions.answer:
Never let unhandled exceptions return raw 500 HTML to API clients. The best approach in .NET 8 is the IExceptionHandler interface:

// .NET 8: IExceptionHandler
public class GlobalExceptionHandler : IExceptionHandler
{
    private readonly ILogger<GlobalExceptionHandler> _logger;
    public GlobalExceptionHandler(ILogger<GlobalExceptionHandler> logger)
        => _logger = logger;

    public async ValueTask<bool> TryHandleAsync(
        HttpContext context,
        Exception exception,
        CancellationToken ct)
    {
        _logger.LogError(exception, "Unhandled exception");

        var (statusCode, title) = exception switch
        {
            KeyNotFoundException     => (404, "Not Found"),
            UnauthorizedAccessException => (403, "Forbidden"),
            ValidationException      => (400, "Validation Error"),
            _                        => (500, "Internal Server Error")
        };

        context.Response.StatusCode = statusCode;
        await context.Response.WriteAsJsonAsync(new ProblemDetails
        {
            Status = statusCode,
            Title = title,
            // Don't expose exception.Message for 5xx in production
            Detail = statusCode < 500 ? exception.Message : null
        }, ct);

        return true;  // exception handled
    }
}

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddExceptionHandler<GlobalExceptionHandler>();
builder.Services.AddProblemDetails();
app.UseExceptionHandler();
followUps: How do you chain multiple exception handlers?

followUpAnswers.What is ProblemDetails (RFC 7807)?:
ProblemDetails is a standardized JSON format for HTTP API error responses, defined in RFC 7807 ("Problem Details for HTTP APIs"). Instead of each API inventing its own error response shape, RFC 7807 defines a machine-readable format that clients can consistently parse.

Standard ProblemDetails fields:

// RFC 7807 fields — all optional but standardized
{
    "type": "https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7231#section-6.5.1",  // URI identifying problem type
    "title": "Bad Request",           // short human-readable summary
    "status": 400,                    // HTTP status code
    "detail": "'email' field is required.",  // specific explanation for this instance
    "instance": "/api/users"          // URI of the specific occurrence
}

// Validation problem details (extends ProblemDetails for 422/400 validation errors)
{
    "type": "https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7231#section-6.5.1",
    "title": "One or more validation errors occurred.",
    "status": 422,
    "errors": {
        "Email": ["The Email field is required."],
        "Age": ["Age must be between 18 and 120."]
    }
}

Using ProblemDetails in ASP.NET Core 8:

// Program.cs — enable ProblemDetails everywhere
builder.Services.AddProblemDetails(options =>
{
    options.CustomizeProblemDetails = ctx =>
    {
        // Add custom fields to every problem response
        ctx.ProblemDetails.Extensions["traceId"] =
            Activity.Current?.Id ?? ctx.HttpContext.TraceIdentifier;
        ctx.ProblemDetails.Extensions["timestamp"] =
            DateTimeOffset.UtcNow.ToString("O");
    };
});

// Returning ProblemDetails from controllers (no custom code needed):
[HttpGet("{id}")]
public async Task<ActionResult<UserDto>> GetUser(int id)
{
    var user = await _repo.FindAsync(id);
    if (user is null)
        return NotFound();  // Automatically returns ProblemDetails with status 404

    return Ok(user);
}

// Creating a custom typed ProblemDetails:
return Problem(
    title: "Insufficient funds",
    detail: $"Account balance ({balance:C}) is less than requested amount ({amount:C})",
    statusCode: 402,
    type: "https://api.example.com/errors/insufficient-funds"
);

Why use ProblemDetails? Clients (mobile apps, SPAs, other microservices) can check content-type: application/problem+json and parse errors uniformly. They don't need to handle a different error shape for each API endpoint. API Management tools and monitoring systems also understand the standard.
followUpAnswers.How do you chain multiple exception hand:
ASP.NET Core .NET 8 supports registering multiple IExceptionHandler implementations. They form a chain — each handler's TryHandleAsync returns true (handled, stop chain) or false (not handled, try next handler).

// Handler 1: domain-specific exceptions (handles known business exceptions)
public class DomainExceptionHandler : IExceptionHandler
{
    private readonly ILogger<DomainExceptionHandler> _logger;
    public DomainExceptionHandler(ILogger<DomainExceptionHandler> logger)
        => _logger = logger;

    public async ValueTask<bool> TryHandleAsync(
        HttpContext context, Exception exception, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        // Only handles domain-specific exceptions
        if (exception is not DomainException domainEx)
            return false;  // Pass to next handler

        _logger.LogWarning(domainEx, "Domain exception: {Code}", domainEx.ErrorCode);

        context.Response.StatusCode = domainEx.HttpStatusCode;
        await context.Response.WriteAsJsonAsync(new ProblemDetails
        {
            Status = domainEx.HttpStatusCode,
            Title = domainEx.ErrorCode,
            Detail = domainEx.Message
        }, ct);

        return true;  // Handled — stop the chain
    }
}

// Handler 2: validation exceptions
public class ValidationExceptionHandler : IExceptionHandler
{
    public async ValueTask<bool> TryHandleAsync(
        HttpContext context, Exception exception, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        if (exception is not ValidationException validationEx)
            return false;  // Not my responsibility — pass to next

        context.Response.StatusCode = 422;
        await context.Response.WriteAsJsonAsync(new ValidationProblemDetails(
            validationEx.Errors
                .GroupBy(e => e.PropertyName)
                .ToDictionary(
                    g => g.Key,
                    g => g.Select(e => e.ErrorMessage).ToArray())), ct);

        return true;
    }
}

// Handler 3: final catch-all — handles everything else
public class GlobalFallbackExceptionHandler : IExceptionHandler
{
    private readonly ILogger<GlobalFallbackExceptionHandler> _logger;
    public GlobalFallbackExceptionHandler(ILogger<GlobalFallbackExceptionHandler> logger)
        => _logger = logger;

    public async ValueTask<bool> TryHandleAsync(
        HttpContext context, Exception exception, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        _logger.LogError(exception, "Unhandled exception");

        context.Response.StatusCode = 500;
        await context.Response.WriteAsJsonAsync(new ProblemDetails
        {
            Status = 500,
            Title = "Internal Server Error"
            // No Detail — don't expose internals
        }, ct);

        return true;  // Always return true in the final catch-all
    }
}

// Program.cs — register in order (first registered = first tried)
builder.Services.AddExceptionHandler<DomainExceptionHandler>();
builder.Services.AddExceptionHandler<ValidationExceptionHandler>();
builder.Services.AddExceptionHandler<GlobalFallbackExceptionHandler>();
builder.Services.AddProblemDetails();
app.UseExceptionHandler();

The framework tries each handler in registration order. The first handler that returns true wins and the chain stops. This pattern is clean, testable (each handler is a separate class), and follows SRP (each handler handles one category of exception).

questions.tip: Don't return stack traces in production. Log the full exception server-side (Serilog → Azure Application Insights), return only a generic ProblemDetails to the client. Returning stack traces is a security vulnerability — attackers use them to learn your server internals.
